relations.note: Both govern first-reference terminology conventions for dermatomyositis-related copy.

quotes: After first mention of "dermatomyositis," use "DM" in running copy to avoid repetition.
Do NOT use "DM" in headlines, headings, or subheads
every standalone section must establish `dermatomyositis (DM)` at its own first mention unless the assembly brief states the abbreviation is established upstream

rules.intent: Ensure DM is always legible/accessible on first encounter regardless of assembly order, while allowing brevity in running body copy.
rules.rule_text: On first mention in running copy, write out "dermatomyositis (DM)" verbatim, then "DM" may be used thereafter to avoid repetition. "DM" must NEVER appear in headlines, headings, or subheads — always spell out the full word there for clarity, accessibility, and SEO. Because sections may be assembled independently, every standalone section must re-establish "dermatomyositis (DM)" at its own first mention unless the assembly brief confirms the abbreviation is already established upstream.

quotes: say "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — not "patients."
("Patients" is acceptable only in HCP materials.)

rules.intent: Keep patient-facing language person-first and warm, reserving clinical terminology for HCP audiences.
rules.rule_text: In patient-facing materials, refer to "someone with dermatomyositis" / "people with dermatomyositis" — never "patients" (that term is reserved for HCP materials). Refer to the surrounding network of patients, HCPs, and support systems as "care team," never "staff."

quotes: Exceptions — always numerals: percentages, ages, ratios.

rules.intent: Keep numeric presentation consistent and scannable across copy.
rules.rule_text: Spell out numbers zero through nine; use numerals for 10 and above. Always use numerals (never spelled out) for percentages, ages, and ratios, and always render percentages with the % sign (e.g., 74%).

quotes: avoid negative or fear-based language; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying
Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo.

rules.intent: Present efficacy data in a way that reassures and informs rather than alarms.
rules.rule_text: When citing clinical/efficacy data, avoid negative or fear-based framing; use an empathetic, supportive, trust-building tone without oversimplifying the science. Illustrative pattern: "Participants in the VALOR study taking LISRAYA had nearly 50% more improvement in their DM symptoms after one year compared to placebo."

quotes: Oxford/serial comma always in series of 3+.

rules.intent: Maintain consistent punctuation style across all copy.
rules.rule_text: Always use the Oxford/serial comma in series of three or more items.
